mappings.value: The time derivative of theta sub i equals omega sub i plus K r times sine of psi minus theta sub i. Here K is the global coupling strength, r and psi are the order-parameter variables from Eq. 2, and each oscillator now feels the same collective mean field instead of separate pairwise couplings.

Equation 3 is the mean-field Kuramoto specialization. After taking K sub i j to be K over N and packaging the population through the complex order parameter, the entire interaction term collapses to one collective pull K r directed toward the mean phase psi.
This is why Eq. 3 is the canonical Kuramoto model: it keeps the phase dynamics but replaces the full coupling matrix with one self-consistent mean field.
Use Eq. 3 when you want the standard mean-field Kuramoto evolution law. It is the natural entry point for the later continuum, stationary-density, and self-consistency equations.
